list: Put the colon on the line after the constructor name, as this cleanly separates the member initializer list from the function prototype.Indent your member initializer list, to make it easier to see the function names.
Indent your member initializer list, to make it easier to see the function names.

content.text: Best practiceMember variables in a member initializer list should be listed in order that they are defined in the class.
Member variables in a member initializer list should be listed in order that they are defined in the class.

list: If a member is listed in the member initializer list, that initialization value is usedOtherwise, if the member has a default member initializer, that initialization value is usedOtherwise, the member is default-initialized.
Otherwise, if the member has a default member initializer, that initialization value is usedOtherwise, the member is default-initialized.

content.text: Key insightOnce the member initializer list has finished executing, the object is considered initialized. Once the function body has finished executing, the object is considered constructed.
Once the member initializer list has finished executing, the object is considered initialized. Once the function body has finished executing, the object is considered constructed.
Best practicePrefer using the member initializer list to initialize your members over assigning values in the body of the constructor.
Prefer using the member initializer list to initialize your members over assigning values in the body of the constructor.
Related contentWe discussed class invariants in lesson14.2 -- Introduction to classes.
We discussed class invariants in lesson14.2 -- Introduction to classes.

list: Resolve the error within the function.Pass the error back to the caller to deal with.Halt the program.Throw an exception.
Pass the error back to the caller to deal with.Halt the program.Throw an exception.

content.text: Key insightThrowing an exception is usually the best thing to do when a constructor fails (and cannot recover). We discuss this further in lessons27.5 -- Exceptions, classes, and inheritanceand27.7 -- Function try blocks.

Throwing an exception is usually the best thing to do when a constructor fails (and cannot recover). We discuss this further in lessons27.5 -- Exceptions, classes, and inheritanceand27.7 -- Function try blocks.
Author’s noteFor now, we’ll generally assume that construction of our class object succeeds in creating a semantically valid object.
For now, we’ll generally assume that construction of our class object succeeds in creating a semantically valid object.

Question #2Why did we makeprint()a non-member function instead of a member function?Show SolutionThe rationale for this is given in lesson14.8 -- The benefits of data hiding (encapsulation).Question #3Why did we makem_colorastd::stringinstead of astd::string_view?Show SolutionIn this particular example, it doesn’t matter (because our color arguments are C-style string literals, which doesn’t go out of scope).But conceptually, we want ourBallclass to be an owner of the color passed in. Ifm_colorwere astd::string_view, passing in a temporary object for the color argument (e.g. astd::stringreturned from a function) would leavem_colordangling when the temporary color argument was destroyed.We discuss this case in more detail (and show an example) in lesson13.11 -- Struct miscellany.Next lesson14.11Default constructors and default argumentsBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson14.9Introduction to constructors
